name: Veterans Memorial Highway
short_description: BLM public land along Nevada's remote Highway 95 corridor offering free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony in high desert terrain. Personal use collecting with no permit required.
description:
Veterans Memorial Highway near Esmeralda County provides excellent opportunities for recreational rockhounders seeking agate and jasper specimens in the remote Great Basin region of Nevada. This BLM-managed public land allows free access for personal collecting with minimal restrictions, making it ideal for both beginners and experienced mineral hunters exploring Nevada's volcanic geology.

The area sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene and Pliocene volcanic activity deposited thick sequences of silica-rich rhyolite and basalt. Weathering and erosion of these volcanic formations have exposed agate nodules, jasper layers, and chalcedony specimens throughout the hillsides and washes adjacent to the highway corridor. The high desert climate has preserved excellent collecting conditions with minimal vegetation obscuring the ground surface.

Rockhounders typically focus on the BLM land west and north of the highway, where sandy washes and bajada slopes yield colorful agate specimens ranging from translucent to opaque, often displaying banding and dendritic patterns. Jasper specimens tend to be larger and more abundant, appearing in shades of red, brown, yellow, and cream. The terrain is relatively open with scattered sagebrush, allowing hikers to cover significant ground and explore multiple wash systems in a single outing.

This location offers the remote, undeveloped character that appeals to rockhounders seeking solitude and authentic field experience. The highway access makes finding parking feasible, though expect minimal facilities and services. Plan for full self-sufficiency including water, fuel, and supplies before departing from Tonopah or Goldfield.
history: This section of the Basin and Range experienced major silica-rich volcanism during the Miocene epoch (23-5 million years ago), when massive ignimbrite eruptions deposited ash flows that welded into rhyolite. Subsequent hydrothermal alteration and groundwater movement concentrated silica into agate nodules and jasper deposits. Small-scale prospecting for minerals occurred throughout Esmeralda County during Nevada's mining booms, though this particular area remains relatively undeveloped and preserved for rockhounding.
tips: Search the sandy and gravelly washes first, where erosion has concentrated lighter agate and jasper pieces on the surface - scan the ground carefully for translucent agate and banded patterns. Check the bajada slopes and hillside exposures where larger jasper masses and colorful specimens are often partially visible in the sandy matrix. Bring a small hand lens to identify chalcedony from quartz and to examine agate banding and color patterns before collecting. Late morning and afternoon light angles are best for spotting translucent specimens that catch and reflect sunlight across the pale desert surface.
terrain_notes: High desert bajada and wash system between 5,400 and 5,800 feet elevation. Terrain is predominantly open sandy slopes with scattered sagebrush and low shrubs, creating unobstructed walking conditions. Washes contain loose rocks and varying depths of sand. Footing is generally stable but can be soft in sandy sections. Minimal shade available. Distances from parking pullouts to productive collecting areas range from 0.25 to 2 miles depending on specific location chosen along the highway.
best_season: October - April. Daytime temperatures in this high desert region are mild and pleasant during fall and spring, with October-November and March-April offering ideal conditions. Winter months can bring occasional snow at this elevation, but most days remain clear and cool. Avoid May through September when afternoon temperatures regularly exceed 90 degrees Fahrenheit and heat becomes a significant hazard in this remote, shadeless terrain.
written_directions: From Tonopah, Nevada, drive south on US 95 for approximately 40 miles toward Beatty. The Veterans Memorial Highway corridor runs through BLM land on both sides of the highway in Esmeralda County. Look for pullouts and BLM access markers along the highway. One primary collecting area is approximately 30 miles south of Tonopah on the west side of US 95, marked by sagebrush flats and sandy washes visible from the road. Park in designated pullouts or safe shoulder areas and walk west into the bajada. Always stay on public BLM land and respect any posted boundary markers.
road_conditions: US 95 is a paved, well-maintained two-lane highway. Pullouts are typically unpaved and may have washboard texture. High-clearance vehicles recommended for deep exploration off main highway pullouts, but passenger cars can safely access primary parking areas.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along US 95 corridor. BLM land allows dispersed parking. Pullouts vary from 2-4 vehicle capacity to larger open areas. No facilities, fees, or reservations. Recommend pulling completely off the highway and parking in designated turnouts to avoid interference with traffic.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection or sale of collected material. No motorized excavation or mechanized collection devices. No collecting within 100 feet of recorded mining claims or private property. Pack out all trash. Leave no trace.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collection
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No motorized excavation or mechanical tools
No collection within 100 feet of active mining claims
Remove all trash and leave no trace
Respect any posted closure or restriction signs
nearest_services: Tonopah, Nevada (approximately 40 miles north) offers gas, food, and lodging. Beatty, Nevada (approximately 40 miles south) also provides services. Carry ample water and supplies before entering this remote area.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain suitable for most hikers. Terrain is mostly open and flat to gently sloping, with soft sand and loose rocks. No steep climbs or technical obstacles. Walking is straightforward across bajada and into washes. Not wheelchair accessible due to sand and uneven surfaces. Sturdy, closed-toe hiking boots recommended to protect feet from sharp material and loose rock. Limited shade and high sun exposure make this accessible for most fitness levels but challenging for those sensitive to heat or direct sun.

faq.question: What agates and jasper can I find rockhounding near Veterans Memorial Highway in Nevada?
faq.answer: Veterans Memorial Highway BLM land produces high-quality agate specimens in translucent and banded varieties, often displaying dendritic patterns and color zoning. Red, brown, yellow, and cream-colored jasper is also abundant, typically in larger pieces. Chalcedony and quartz are common secondary finds. Most specimens are collectible within the 25 lb daily limit.

faq.question: Is there a permit or fee required for rockhounding Veterans Memorial Highway, Nevada?
faq.answer: No permit or fee is required. This is BLM public land open for free recreational collecting under FLPMA regulations. Personal use collection of up to 25 lbs per day is allowed without advance permits or reservations. No commercial use is permitted.

faq.question: How do I find agates and jasper at Veterans Memorial Highway rockhounding area?
faq.answer: Focus your search on sandy washes where erosion has concentrated lighter agate and jasper pieces on the surface. Scan bajada slopes and hillside exposures where larger specimens are partially visible in the sandy matrix. Use afternoon sunlight to spot translucent agates that catch light. Check areas where water has recently flowed, as these natural drainages concentrate heavier mineral material.

faq.question: What is the best time to rockhound Veterans Memorial Highway near Tonopah?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal when daytime temperatures are mild and comfortable. Fall and spring (October-November and March-April) offer perfect conditions. Avoid May through September when afternoon temperatures exceed 90 degrees Fahrenheit in this shadeless, remote high desert terrain and heat becomes a serious hazard.

faq.question: Is Veterans Memorial Highway rockhounding area suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, this is beginner-friendly terrain. The open bajada and wash systems require no technical skills or rock climbing. Children ages 6 and up can participate safely with adult supervision. The flat, accessible terrain and abundance of surface specimens make it excellent for teaching rockhounding basics. Bring ample water, sunscreen, and hats due to intense sun exposure.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Veterans Memorial Highway, Nevada?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens to identify chalcedony and examine agate banding, a small backpack, cloth bags or buckets for specimens, sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support, sunscreen, a wide-brimmed hat, and at least 3-4 liters of water per person. Pack a GPS unit or smartphone with offline maps since cell service is spotty. No motorized tools or excavation equipment is permitted.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony near Tonopah, Nevada. No permit required. 25 lb daily limit. Remote high desert collecting.
